title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Say It with Flowers: The Secret Language of Floriography
content.text: In Victorian England, sending flowers was rarely a straightforward act of generosity. Each bloom carried a specific coded meaning, and a carefully assembled bouquet could convey declarations of love, expressions of jealousy, or even thinly veiled insults — all without a single word being spoken. (37) [37] Este lenguaje floral tenía reglas precisas y complejas. [/37] The system was elaborate enough that gentlemen and ladies kept reference books beside them, consulting the meanings of individual flowers before composing or interpreting a floral message. || The practice known as floriography did not originate in Britain. It was introduced to Western Europe in the early eighteenth century by Lady Mary Wortley Montagu, who encountered it during her time in Constantinople and wrote enthusiastically about it in her correspondence. (38) [38] El origen del lenguaje de las flores no era europeo. [/38] The Turks, she reported, had developed a sophisticated vocabulary of objects — not just flowers, but also fruits, spices and fabrics — each carrying a distinct coded meaning. || In Victorian Britain, floriography reached its peak of popularity, fuelled by the publication of dozens of flower-meaning dictionaries. The red rose, inevitably, meant love; the yellow rose suggested jealousy or infidelity. (39) [39] Cada flor tenía un significado preciso y reconocible. [/39] Lavender stood for devotion, ivy for fidelity, and the snapdragon — perhaps surprisingly — conveyed deception. Not all sources agreed on the precise meanings, which occasionally led to comic misunderstandings. || The meanings assigned to flowers were not always consistent across different countries. A flower that symbolised innocence in England might carry connotations of mourning in France. (40) [40] Las diferencias culturales complicaban la comunicación floral internacional. [/40] Diplomats and travellers needed to exercise considerable caution when exchanging bouquets across borders. || Interest in floriography declined sharply at the turn of the twentieth century as communication became more direct and the elaborate social codes of the Victorian era fell out of fashion. (41) [41] El lenguaje de las flores cayó en desuso en el siglo XX. [/41] Wedding florists, perfumers and greeting card companies began referencing Victorian flower meanings in their marketing, and a new generation of enthusiasts began compiling updated guides. || Today, floriography occupies a curious place between history and living practice. (42) [42] Su legado persiste en la cultura popular contemporánea. [/42] Films, novels and social media accounts dedicated to flower symbolism continue to attract audiences who enjoy the idea of a hidden language growing quietly in gardens and florists' shops around the world.

paragraphs.A: A bouquet sent without this knowledge risked conveying entirely the wrong sentiment.
paragraphs.B: This was not a European invention but an import from the Ottoman world, arriving via a well-travelled aristocratic correspondent.
paragraphs.C: These inconsistencies meant that a single bunch of flowers could be read in entirely different ways depending on the recipient's cultural background.
paragraphs.D: Moss rose indicated confessed love, while a striped carnation conveyed a firm refusal.
paragraphs.E: However, a revival of interest began in the late twentieth century, spurred partly by nostalgia and partly by the growing popularity of vintage aesthetics.
paragraphs.F: Its traces survive in idiom, in literature, and in the persistent association of certain flowers with particular emotions.
paragraphs.G: The practice faded so thoroughly that by mid-century most people were unaware it had ever existed.

questions.explanation: A reinforces the complexity of the system and the need to consult books before sending or interpreting bouquets.
B introduces the Ottoman origin of the language of flowers through Lady Montagu, explained in detail below.
D adds more examples of floral meanings (moss rose, striped carnation) continuing the list started with the red rose and lavender.
C concludes the problem of cultural differences by explaining how the same bouquet could be interpreted in different ways.
E introduces the resurgence of interest in the twentieth century, which links to the modern use by florists and greeting cards.
F describes the residual presence of floral language in language and literature, supporting the final paragraph on its cultural legacy.
